Pituitary–Adrenal Responses and Glucocorticoid Receptor Expression in Critically Ill Patients with COVID-19

evidence_entities.text: The hypothalamus–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis was described as the principal component of the stress response 85 years ago, along with the acute-phase reaction, and the defense response at the tissue level.

Apart from its beneficial cardiovascular and metabolic effects, cortisol exerts a significant immunoregulatory role, a major attribute being that it restrains the excessive inflammatory reaction, thereby preventing unwanted tissue damage.
Chronic stimulation with ACTH leads to hyperplasia and hypertrophy of the adrenal glands.

CIRCI is characterized by an inadequate production of cortisol in relation to increased demands during periods of severe stress and is accompanied by an exaggerated and protracted pro-inflammatory response.

In fact, the presence of both ACE2 and TMPRSS2 has been documented in many endocrine tissues, including the adrenal zonae fasciculata and reticularis, as well as the hypothalamus and pituitary, making them possible direct targets of SARS-CoV-2.

GCR-α is the functionally active receptor that binds to cortisol. After binding, the GCR–cortisol complex translocates from the cytosol to the nucleus, where the complex exerts transcriptional activation or repression by directly binding to genes that contain GC responsive elements.
On the contrary, GCR-β has not been well-explored. It is well-known to suppress GCR-α activity and is unable to bind natural or synthetic ligands.
